conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to gather some information about your circumstances.

Let me start by asking: Do you have any children under the age of 20 who you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I've got three children actually - Alex who's 20, Blake who's 16, and Charlie who's 5. I live in Leeds with Alex and Charlie, but Blake lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to understand a bit more about each child's situation to assess eligibility properly.

Let me start with Alex who is 20 - is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or certain vocational courses)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They've actually been in hospital for a few months now - had some health issues that needed treatment. I still pop in regularly and bring them bits and pieces they need, you know, magazines, snacks, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I'm sorry to hear about Alex's health issues.

Regarding Alex's hospital stay - is this an NHS hospital, and has Alex been there continuously for more than 12 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's an NHS hospital. They've been in there for about 13 weeks now - it's been a tough few months for all of us, honestly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand that must be very difficult for your family.

Now, let me ask about Blake who is 16 and lives with their other parent. Do you contribute at least the same amount toward Blake's upkeep as the amount of Child Benefit would be (currently around £25 per week for a child who isn't the eldest), or do you and Blake's other parent contribute equally to their upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: To be honest, I'm not contributing anything financially towards Blake at the moment. Their other parent handles all of that since Blake lives with them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. And just to confirm about Blake - do they live with their other parent full-time, or do they spend time living with you as well?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with their other parent full-time. They don't stay with me at all really.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Now, regarding Charlie who is 5 and lives with you - is Charlie living with you full-time, and are you responsible for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie lives with me full-time. They're only 5, so they're just in primary school and I take care of everything for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your three children.

Based on the information you've provided and the UK government rules, here's my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment of your circumstances against the UK government's Child Benefit rules:

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.**

**Summary for each child:**

- **Alex (20)**: Not eligible - Alex is over the age limit as they are 20 and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit only continues past 16 if the child is under 20 and in approved education or training.

- **Blake (16)**: Not eligible - Blake lives full-time with their other parent and you're not contributing financially towards their upkeep. The other parent would be entitled to claim Child Benefit for Blake as they are the one responsible for them.

- **Charlie (5)**: **Eligible** - Charlie is under 16, lives with you full-time, and you're responsible for their care. You meet all the requirements for Child Benefit.

You can apply for Child Benefit for Charlie if you haven't already. If you have any questions about how to claim, I'd be happy to help further.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 20, which is 20 or over

Child lives with claimant
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child is not fostered

expected_eligibility.name: Charlie
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child lives with claimant

Child does not work 24+ hours/week
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training, which means they exceed the age limit for Child Benefit (under 20 and in approved education/training, or under 16). Although Alex has been in an NHS hospital for 13 weeks, the rules state that after 12 weeks in hospital, you're usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless you regularly spend money on them. While you visit and bring magazines and snacks, this level of spending may not meet the threshold required for the exception to apply. Additionally, Alex is over the age limit regardless of the hospital stay.

Blake is 16 and lives full-time with their other parent. You are not contributing financially towards Blake's upkeep. Under the rules, you would normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child - either living with them OR paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since Blake lives with the other parent full-time and you're not making financial contributions equivalent to Child Benefit, you do not meet the eligibility criteria. The other parent would be the one entitled to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old (under 16), lives with you full-time, and you are responsible for their care. You meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie as you are responsible for a child under 16 who lives with you in the UK.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie only. You are not eligible for Alex (over age limit and not in approved education/training) or Blake (lives with other parent full-time and you don't contribute financially to their upkeep).

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training, which means they exceed the age limit for Child Benefit (under 20 and in approved education/training, or under 16). Although Alex has been in an NHS hospital for 13 weeks, the rules state that after 12 weeks in hospital, you're usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless you regularly spend money on them. While you visit and bring magazines and snacks, this level of spending may not meet the threshold required for the exception to apply. Additionally, Alex is over the age limit regardless of the hospital stay.

Blake is 16 and lives full-time with their other parent. You are not contributing financially towards Blake's upkeep. Under the rules, you would normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child - either living with them OR paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since Blake lives with the other parent full-time and you're not making financial contributions equivalent to Child Benefit, you do not meet the eligibility criteria. The other parent would be the one entitled to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old (under 16), lives with you full-time, and you are responsible for their care. You meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie as you are responsible for a child under 16 who lives with you in the UK.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie only. You are not eligible for Alex (over age limit and not in approved education/training) or Blake (lives with other parent full-time and you don't contribute financially to their upkeep).
